epidemiology: Common in children in tropical and temperate regions, especially in low-resource settings with poor hygiene.
treatment: Clean with antiseptic. Topical mupirocin 2% TID for 5-7 days. If extensive, oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg BID for 7 days.
referral_criteria: No improvement after 48 hours of treatment, signs of cellulitis, or recurrent episodes.
prevention: Improve hygiene, wash with soap and water, trim nails, avoid sharing towels.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list: mupirocin or fusidic acid for localized impetigo.

cause: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep), Staphylococcus aureus
epidemiology: Occurs worldwide, risk increases with breaks in skin (wounds, ulcers, fungal infections). Common in diabetic patients.
treatment: Oral antibiotics: cephalexin 500 mg QID or cloxacillin 500 mg QID for 7-10 days. Severe cases require IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone).
referral_criteria: Signs of systemic infection (fever, chills, tachycardia), rapid spread, facial involvement, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Treat predisposing conditions (tinea pedis, wounds), moisturize dry skin to prevent cracking.
who_guideline: WHO IMCI guidelines recommend referral for any red flag signs in children.

epidemiology: Common in warm, humid tropical climates. Affects all ages. Spread by direct contact or fomites.
treatment: Topical terbinafine 1% once daily for 2 weeks OR clotrimazole 1% BID for 4 weeks. Widespread cases: oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 2-4 weeks.
referral_criteria: Extensive or resistant cases, immunosuppressed patients, uncertain diagnosis.
prevention: Keep skin dry, avoid sharing clothing/towels, treat affected household members.
who_guideline: WHO recommends topical azoles or terbinafine as first-line treatment.

epidemiology: Risk increases with age (>50 years) and immunosuppression. More common in tropics where childhood varicella is universal.
treatment: Oral acyclovir 800 mg 5x daily for 7 days (start within 72 hours). Pain management: paracetamol, NSAIDs, or gabapentin for post-herpetic neuralgia.
referral_criteria: Ophthalmic involvement (eye), disseminated rash, immunocompromised patient, severe pain.
prevention: Vaccination (live attenuated zoster vaccine recommended for adults >50 years).
who_guideline: WHO recommends acyclovir for treatment, especially in immunocompromised and elderly patients.

cause: Leishmania tropica, L. major, L. braziliensis (protozoan parasite transmitted by sandflies)
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical and subtropical regions: Africa, Middle East, Central/South America, Indian subcontinent. Rural areas.
treatment: Intralesional sodium stibogluconate or topical paromomycin 15%/gentamicin 0.5% BID for 20 days. Systemic therapy for complex cases.
referral_criteria: Multiple lesions (>5), large lesions (>5 cm), mucosal involvement, treatment failure.
prevention: Insecticide-treated bed nets, repellents, sandfly control programs.
who_guideline: WHO recommends intralesional antimonials or topical paromomycin as first-line for uncomplicated cutaneous leishmaniasis.

epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, Southeast Asia, and Pacific Islands. Affects children 2-15 years in poor rural communities.
treatment: Single dose azithromycin 30 mg/kg oral (max 2 g) or benzathine penicillin 1.2 million units IM.
referral_criteria: Suspected tertiary yaws (bone/joint involvement), treatment failure after 4 weeks.
prevention: WHO yaws eradication campaign: mass drug administration with azithromycin in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO recommends single-dose azithromycin as first-line treatment for yaws eradication.

epidemiology: West Africa (especially Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Benin), also parts of Asia and Australia. Slow-growing, painless ulcer.
treatment: Rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily + clarithromycin 7.5 mg/kg BID or moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 8 weeks.
referral_criteria: All confirmed cases should be referred for treatment. WHO recommends referral to specialized Buruli ulcer treatment centers.
prevention: Wear protective clothing in endemic areas, avoid contact with contaminated water sources.
who_guideline: WHO recommends 8-week antibiotic therapy (rifampicin + clarithromycin) followed by surgery if needed.

epidemiology: Common in overcrowded conditions worldwide, especially tropical regions. High prevalence in children and institutional settings.
treatment: Permethrin 5% cream applied head-to-toe overnight, repeated after 7 days. Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose (repeat after 7 days).
referral_criteria: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies, secondary infection with cellulitis, treatment failure.
prevention: Treat all household contacts simultaneously, wash bedding in hot water, avoid close contact until treated.
who_guideline: WHO NTD roadmap includes scabies control. Ivermectin mass drug administration in endemic communities.

epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, India, and Central America. 'Transplantation belt' between 15°S and 30°N. Most common in barefoot agricultural workers.
treatment: Eumycetoma: Itraconazole 400 mg daily for 12+ months + surgery. Actinomycetoma: Streptomycin 14 mg/kg IM daily + dapsone 1.5 mg/kg BID for months.
referral_criteria: All suspected mycetoma should be referred. Requires specialized care for prolonged treatment.
prevention: Wear shoes/protective footwear when working outdoors in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO recognizes mycetoma as a neglected tropical disease. Long-term antifungal therapy essential for eumycetoma.

epidemiology: Endemic in Southeast Asia (especially Thailand, Vietnam) and northern Australia. Associated with rice paddies and wet soil.
treatment: Intensive phase: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg QID or meropenem 25 mg/kg TID for 14+ days. Eradication: TMP-SMX 8/40 mg/kg BID for 12-20 weeks.
referral_criteria: All suspected cases require immediate referral for IV antibiotics. High mortality if untreated.
prevention: Avoid contact with soil and water in endemic areas, wear protective footwear, treat wounds promptly.
who_guideline: WHO recommends hospitalization for intensive IV antibiotic therapy followed by prolonged oral eradication.

epidemiology: Endemic in India, Brazil, Indonesia, and parts of Africa. Transmission via respiratory droplets. Long incubation period (2-10 years).
treatment: Paucibacillary: Rifampicin 600 mg monthly + dapsone 100 mg daily for 6 months. Multibacillary: Add clofazimine 300 mg monthly, 50 mg daily for 12 months.
referral_criteria: All confirmed or suspected cases should be referred to a leprosy control program for treatment and monitoring.
prevention: BCG vaccination provides some protection. Contact screening in endemic areas. WHO provides free MDT.
who_guideline: WHO-recommended multidrug therapy (MDT) is the standard of care, provided free through WHO.

cause: Dengue virus (DENV-1-4, flavivirus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Endemic in >100 tropical countries. Major cause of fever with rash in Southeast Asia, Latin America, and Africa.
treatment: Supportive care: paracetamol for fever (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin due to bleeding risk). Monitor for warning signs (abdominal pain, vomiting, mucosal bleeding).
referral_criteria: Any warning sign, rising hematocrit with rapid platelet drop, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, lethargy.
prevention: Mosquito control (eliminate breeding sites), insect repellent, bed nets. Dengue vaccine (CYD-TDV) available for seropositive individuals.
who_guideline: WHO dengue classification: dengue without warning signs, dengue with warning signs, severe dengue.

cause: Chikungunya virus (alphavirus, transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Epidemics in Africa, Asia, Indian subcontinent, and the Americas. Often co-circulates with dengue.
treatment: Supportive care: paracetamol, NSAIDs for joint pain (after excluding dengue). Rest and physical therapy for chronic joint symptoms.
referral_criteria: Severe joint pain limiting mobility, persistent symptoms beyond 3 weeks, neurological signs.
prevention: Same as dengue: mosquito avoidance, repellents, bed nets. No specific vaccine widely available.
who_guideline: WHO emphasizes symptomatic treatment. No specific antiviral treatment available.

epidemiology: Outbreaks in unvaccinated populations worldwide. High mortality in malnourished children in developing countries.
treatment: Supportive care: vitamin A 200,000 IU daily for 2 days (reduces mortality). Treat secondary infections with appropriate antibiotics.
referral_criteria: Severe pneumonia, encephalitis, severe dehydration, malnutrition, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: MMR vaccine (two doses). WHO recommends >95% vaccination coverage for herd immunity.
who_guideline: WHO recommends vitamin A for all children with measles in settings where deficiency is common.

cause: Multifactorial: genetic (filaggrin mutation), environmental, immune dysregulation
epidemiology: Common worldwide, increasing in developing countries. Onset usually in early childhood.
treatment: Emollients regularly. Topical hydrocortisone 1% for mild, topical tacrolimus for face. Treat secondary infection with antibiotics.
referral_criteria: Severe refractory disease, suspected eczema herpeticum, significant impact on quality of life.
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoid triggers (soaps, allergens), wear soft cotton clothing.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list includes topical corticosteroids for eczema treatment.

cause: Autoimmune (T-cell mediated), genetic predisposition (HLA-Cw6), triggered by infections, stress, medications
epidemiology: Prevalence 0.5-3% worldwide, lower in tropical regions. Peaks at ages 20-30 and 50-60.
treatment: Topical: vitamin D analogs, corticosteroids, coal tar. Systemic for severe: methotrexate, phototherapy, biologics.
referral_criteria: Severe/extensive disease, psoriatic arthritis, treatment failure with topical therapy.
prevention: Avoid triggers: stress, beta-blockers, lithium, alcohol. Maintain skin hydration.
who_guideline: WHO Global Report on Psoriasis highlights need for access to treatment in low-resource settings.

cause: Mast cell degranulation from allergens, infections, medications, physical triggers (heat, cold, pressure)
epidemiology: Affects 15-25% of population at least once. Acute (<6 weeks) vs chronic (>6 weeks).
treatment: Antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, loratadine 10 mg). Severe: short course oral prednisolone 30-40 mg daily for 3-5 days.
referral_criteria: Chronic urticaria (>6 weeks), angioedema without wheals, suspected autoimmune cause.
prevention: Avoid known triggers, keep a diary to identify triggers.
who_guideline: WHO model formulary includes oral antihistamines as first-line treatment.

epidemiology: Very common in tropical climates due to heat and humidity. Affects all ages.
treatment: Topical terbinafine 1% or clotrimazole 1% BID for 2-4 weeks. Oral terbinafine for scalp/nail involvement.
referral_criteria: Extensive involvement, scalp infection (tinea capitis), nail infection, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Keep skin dry, wear breathable fabrics, avoid sharing towels, treat athlete's foot promptly.
who_guideline: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines includes clotrimazole and terbinafine.

cause: Irritant (chemical, soap) or allergic (nickel, poison ivy, latex) reaction
epidemiology: Common worldwide, accounts for 4-7% of dermatology visits. Occupational dermatitis common in tropical agriculture.
treatment: Remove offending agent. Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1% for mild, betamethasone for severe). Antihistamines for itching.
referral_criteria: Severe or widespread reaction, suspected occupational cause requiring patch testing, secondary infection.
prevention: Identify and avoid triggers, use barrier creams/protective gloves for occupational exposure.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines: hydrocortisone cream for inflammatory skin conditions.

epidemiology: Universal childhood infection in unvaccinated populations. More severe in adults and immunocompromised.
treatment: Supportive care: calamine lotion, antihistamines, paracetamol for fever. Acyclovir for severe cases or at-risk patients.
referral_criteria: Immunocompromised patient, severe disease, pneumonitis, encephalitis, secondary bacterial infection.
prevention: Varicella vaccine (routine childhood immunization).
who_guideline: WHO recommends varicella vaccination for routine childhood immunization programs.

cause: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep) producing erythrogenic toxin
epidemiology: Primarily affects children 5-15 years. Outbreaks in schools and communities worldwide.
treatment: Penicillin V 250 mg BID for 10 days or amoxicillin 50 mg/kg daily for 10 days. Treat household contacts if symptomatic.
referral_criteria: Signs of complicated disease: rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar abscess.
prevention: Good hygiene, cover mouth when coughing. No vaccine available.
who_guideline: WHO recommends penicillin as first-line treatment for group A streptococcal infections.

epidemiology: Common in children worldwide. Spread by direct contact and fomites. More prevalent in tropical climates.
treatment: Usually self-resolving in 6-12 months. Options: cryotherapy, curettage, cantharidin, topical imiquimod.
referral_criteria: Immunocompromised patient (HIV), extensive disease, uncertain diagnosis.
prevention: Avoid direct contact with lesions, do not share towels, good hand hygiene.
who_guideline: WHO notes it is common in HIV-positive patients and can be more extensive.

cause: Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi (filarial nematodes transmitted by mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, Asia, Western Pacific, and parts of Latin America. Leading cause of permanent disability worldwide.
treatment: Annual mass drug administration: ivermectin + albendazole (plus DEC in non-onchocerciasis areas). Acute attacks: antibiotics for secondary infection.
referral_criteria: Acute adenolymphangitis, secondary cellulitis, hydrocele requiring surgery.
prevention: Mosquito control, insecticide-treated bed nets, annual MDA in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis uses annual MDA as primary strategy.
